sit: You're the puck carrier entering the offensive zone. One defender is set at the blue line right in front of you. What's the right decision?
badOpt: Dump it in because your teammates need time to set up and look for support.
correctOpt: Read the defender — if they're set, use your linemate or chip it behind them.
why: A set defender at the blue line is telling you something. Read them — if they're set and you try to carry, you risk a turnover in the worst spot. Use your linemate or chip the puck in.

sit: You're the center on a 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. What do you do?
badOpt: Dump it in since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
why: When both defenders go wide to cover the wings, they've given you the middle. Drive it and shoot — the goalie is unscreened and the lane is open.

sit: Your team is on the power play. You have the extra skater. What's the most important thing your team should be doing with that advantage?
badOpt: Drop back and play defense since the other team has committed to the other side instead.
correctOpt: Move the puck until you find the open player — the extra man means someone is always open.
why: The power play advantage is the extra skater. Moving the puck until you find who isn't covered exploits that advantage.

sit: An attacker is coming at you 1-on-1 at the top of your defensive zone. What's the right approach?
badOpt: Get your stick in the lane since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Back up at a controlled pace, angle them toward the boards, and wait for their first move.
why: Rushing a skilled attacker gives them the chance to blow past you. Backing up at a controlled gap while angling forces them to go where you want.

sit: Your D picks up the puck. One forechecker is closing from the left. Your right winger is up the wall on the right — open. Your left winger is covered. What's the breakout call?
badOpt: Protect the puck there since the other team has committed to the other side early.
correctOpt: Pass to the open right winger — the forechecker has committed to the left side.
why: The forechecker committing to one side creates an opening on the other side. Read the forechecker and use the open outlet.

sit: Two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. They have speed. What's the first thing you do?
badOpt: Hold your position at the crease since the other team has committed to the other side instead.
correctOpt: Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter.
why: Speed on a 2-on-1 is the attacker's advantage. Backing up fast enough while staying in the middle takes away the pass and forces the shot.

sit: You're the puck carrier in the neutral zone at full speed. Two defenders are set at the blue line. Your linemate is trailing. Best entry?
badOpt: Skate to the corner and dig it out since the other team has committed to the other side instead.
correctOpt: Chip the puck past the inside defender and go get it, or use a give-and-go with your trailer.
why: Carrying against two set defenders at the blue line is low-percentage. The chip-and-chase or give-and-go with the trailer are the smart options.

sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. The play breaks down and the other team gets the puck. You're the center, close to the play. What do you do first?
badOpt: Change lines because your teammates need time to set up again.
correctOpt: Pressure the puck carrier immediately to slow the breakout.
why: When a rush breaks down, the nearest forward pressures the puck immediately. Slowing their first move buys your teammates two seconds to recover.

sit: You're about to shoot from the slot. The goalie is leaning hard to your right. Where should you shoot?
badOpt: Pull up at the hash marks because your teammates need time to set up on the play.
correctOpt: Low left — the opposite side of where they're leaning. They can't recover in time.
why: Pre-shot reads of the goalie give you a target before you release. A goalie leaning hard to one side is leaving the other side vulnerable.

sit: You're on a breakaway. The goalie is skating aggressively out to cut your angle. What's the most effective play?
badOpt: Pass it to your D at the far point because your teammates need time to set up right away.
correctOpt: Pull the puck wide, make the goalie commit to one side, then shoot or go to the other side.
why: An aggressive goalie is looking for an early shot or for you to go straight. Make them commit — take the puck wide, let them follow, then shoot or go opposite.

sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure. The puck is won behind your net. Two forecheckers are pinching in from both sides. What breakout option gives you the best chance?
badOpt: Carry it wide since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover instead.
correctOpt: Reverse the puck to the other side — one forechecker can't follow, buying time for your winger to get position.
why: Under heavy forecheck pressure with two pinching forecheckers, the reverse gives you an extra second. One forechecker can't cover both sides simultaneously.

sit: You're at the half-wall with the puck. The slot defender is watching the corner. Nobody is tight on you. What's the play?
badOpt: Drop back and play defense because your teammates need time to set up.
correctOpt: Shoot from the half-wall — you have time and the slot defender is distracted.
why: When the slot defender is watching the corner and nobody's tight on you at the half-wall, you have a shooting opportunity. Take it.

sit: Your team has been cycling for 30 seconds. The defenders have perfectly matched your cycle. Nothing is opening. What do you do?
badOpt: Force a play through traffic because your teammates need time to set up instead.
correctOpt: Stop the cycle — dump the puck deep, change lines, and come back with a new entry.
why: A cycle against a perfectly matched defense that isn't creating anything is burning your legs. Recognize when the cycle isn't working and come in with a different look.

sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1. The puck is at the top. A penalty killer closes hard on the top player. What should the top player do?
badOpt: Pass it to your D at the far point because your teammates need time to set up on the play.
correctOpt: Move the puck quickly before the pressure arrives — pass to the half-wall or the other side.
why: On the power play, moving the puck before pressure arrives is the key. One extra skater means someone is always open — find them before the PK player gets there.

sit: You're on the penalty kill. The power play is cycling below the dots. They're not shooting — just moving the puck. When should you challenge?
badOpt: Get your stick in the lane because your teammates need time to set up.
correctOpt: When they slow down or seem to be setting up a shot — not on every pass.
why: On the PK, challenging on every pass runs you out of position. Pick your moments — challenge when the puck carrier is stationary or loading up.

sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
badOpt: Force a play through traffic since the other team has committed to the other side right away.
correctOpt: Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance.
why: Late-game puck management is a skill. Cycling below the dots burns clock, forces the other team to chase, and keeps the puck away from their skilled players.

sit: Your team just had a power play chance and the goalie covered the puck. Faceoff in the offensive zone. You win the draw. Two of the other team's forwards are already breaking out. What's the immediate play?
badOpt: Pass it to your D at the far point since the other team has committed to the other side right away.
correctOpt: Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP.
why: Reading a counter-opportunity after a dead puck is advanced hockey sense. If two forwards are breaking and you win the draw, the counter is a better chance than resetting the power play.

sit: You're the offensive zone D at the blue line. Your forward is about to lose possession of the puck in the corner to a defender who can break with speed. Should you pinch?
badOpt: Try to stickhandle through them because your teammates need time to set up while staying in position.
correctOpt: No — if possession is about to be lost, pinching puts you deep with no coverage during a fast breakout.
why: The moment possession is about to be lost is the worst time to pinch. If the D gets the puck clean, they'll have a speed advantage with you caught deep.

sit: You're the D at the point. You see the opposing center beginning to break toward your blue line for a potential counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
badOpt: Force a play through traffic since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready to pursue if the puck turns over.
why: Reading a player beginning to position for a counter lets you cheat back slightly. You stay at the line but you're ready. If the puck turns, you're in position.

sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone trap. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
badOpt: Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover again.
correctOpt: Pull the puck back to your D, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement — go backward to go forward.

sit: You're the last defender back as two attackers come in on you. One has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
badOpt: Skate hard since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
why: Last man back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass, force the shot, trust your goalie. Don't commit to either attacker until they force your hand.

sit: Two attackers entered your zone and both D picked them up. A third attacker trails in 5 seconds later — nobody picked them up. You're the center on defense. What do you do?
badOpt: Drive to the net front because your teammates need time to set up and look for support.
correctOpt: Pick up the late attacker — your winger on that side needs to find their assignment.
why: A late attacker entering the zone creates a coverage breakdown. The center needs to pick them up or communicate a switch immediately.

sit: Your team just finished a power play chance and the goalie covered the puck. You're a forward who just won the faceoff in the offensive zone. You see two opponents already breaking out. What's the immediate play?
badOpt: Pass it to your D at the far point because your teammates need time to set up and look for support.
correctOpt: Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP.
why: Reading a counter-opportunity after a dead puck is advanced hockey sense. If two forwards are breaking and you win the draw, the counter is a better chance than resetting.

sit: You're in tight to the net — five feet out. The goalie is set. You have the puck on your backhand but barely any time. What do you do?
badOpt: Carry it wide since the other team has committed to the other side at that point.
correctOpt: Release your backhand immediately — the goalie is set for the forehand anyway.
why: In tight to the net with time running out, a quick backhand release is often better than taking the time to switch to your forehand. The goalie is already set for the forehand.

sit: Two forecheckers are deep in your zone. Your winger is up the wall but covered. Your center in the middle is also covered. Your D has the puck behind the net. What's the best option?
badOpt: Drop back and play defense since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Wheel and carry it out — both forecheckers are committed to your forwards. The D carrying it out is now the best option.
why: When both forecheckers have committed to your forwards and left the D unguarded, the D carrying it out is the right play.

sit: Your team breaks out. As you cross the red line you see both opposing defensemen are pinching up — they're at the red line, not their blue line. What do you do?
badOpt: Skate hard because your teammates need time to set up while staying in position.
correctOpt: Skate hard behind them — you have a breakaway if you beat them to their blue line.
why: Defensemen pinching up means they've given up the space behind them. If you can beat them to the puck, you have a breakaway or a clean zone entry.

sit: Your team is breaking out. The strong-side winger is completely covered. The D picks up the puck on the right side. The center is in the middle and the left winger is open on the short side. What's the right read?
badOpt: Change lines since the other team has committed to the other side at that point.
correctOpt: Pass to the open left winger on the short side — shortest distance, cleanest pass.
why: The short-side winger being open is the clean, low-risk breakout pass. The shortest distance pass under pressure is usually the right one.

sit: Your team's power play is struggling. The PK has collapsed everything to the slot and the point. What adjustment do you make?
badOpt: Hold your position at the crease since the other team has committed to the other side at that point.
correctOpt: Pull the puck back out of the zone, reset, and come in with a new entry that attacks a different spot.
why: When the PK has your power play figured out and collapsed, pull back and re-enter with a different attack angle. Don't keep running the same play into a locked door.

sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. You're the center taking a defensive zone faceoff. The power play wants to win the draw back to the point for a shot setup. What's your goal on this draw?
badOpt: Swing wide along the boards since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Win it forward — kick it toward the corner or boards to prevent the PP shot setup.
why: On a PK defensive zone faceoff, winning it forward — toward the boards or corner — takes away the PP's plan. Winning backward gives the PP exactly what they want.

sit: Three attackers are rushing at your two defensemen. All three are at top speed. What's the biggest mistake a defenseman can make here?
badOpt: Hold the puck since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Backing up at the right controlled pace, not overcommitting.
why: Pinching up against a fast rush hands the attacking team a massive speed advantage. Backing up at the right controlled pace is the correct response.

sit: Your team just finished a power play. The puck is stopped and the penalty expires. Now your team is 5-on-5 but the other team's defensemen are still pinched in your offensive zone. What do you do?
badOpt: Pressure the carrier hard because your teammates need time to set up and look for support.
correctOpt: Exploit the pinched D immediately — move the puck quick and attack before they recover.
why: When the other team's D are caught pinched at the end of your power play, the transition to 5-on-5 creates an immediate odd-man opportunity. Attack before they recover.

sit: Your team is forechecking and the other team wins a battle in the corner. They're about to break out with speed. You're the high forward. What's the most important thing you do right now?
badOpt: Drive to the net front because your teammates need time to set up.
correctOpt: Hold your position and intercept the first outlet pass — you're the plug.
why: When the puck changes in the corner, the high forward's job is to be the first interceptor — not to chase. Holding position and reading the breakout gives you a chance to pick off the first pass.

sit: Your team just won a puck battle in your own zone. Two of your forwards are already at center ice. The other team has one player back. A 3-on-1 is developing. What's the trigger?
badOpt: Pass back since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Go immediately — the 3-on-1 window closes fast as the other team gets back.
why: Transition opportunities close fast. If two forwards are already at center and you have a 3-on-1 developing, going immediately is how you exploit it.

sit: Your team breaks out. As you cross the red line you see both opposing defensemen are pinching up — they're at the red line, not their blue line. What do you do?
badOpt: Get your stick in the lane because your teammates need time to set up.
correctOpt: Skate hard behind them — you have a breakaway if you beat them to their blue line.
why: Defensemen pinching up means they've given up the space behind them. If you can beat them to the puck, you have a breakaway or a clean zone entry.

sit: You're a defenseman in your own zone. The opposing winger has the puck in the corner and keeps looking at the player in the slot. What do you do?
badOpt: Change lines since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Step in front of the anticipated pass lane — intercept it before it arrives.
why: Reading a puck carrier's eyes tells you where the pass is going. If they keep looking at the slot, get in that lane before the pass is made — not after.

sit: You're cycling below the dots. Your partner passes to you at the half-wall. As soon as the puck hits your stick, you see the slot is completely empty. What's the play?
badOpt: Carry it wide because your teammates need time to set up from this spot.
correctOpt: Shoot immediately into the open slot or pass to a teammate breaking there.
why: The slot opening up is exactly what the cycle is designed to create. The instant you see it, attack it — don't keep cycling for its own sake.

sit: The other team just changed lines mid-play in the neutral zone. Their new players are still getting up to speed. You have the puck. What does this create?
badOpt: Drop back and play defense since the other team has committed to the other side while staying in position.
correctOpt: A transition window — attack immediately while their fresh players aren't at full speed or in position.
why: A line change mid-play creates a brief window where the fresh players aren't at full speed or position. Attacking immediately exploits that window before it closes.

sit: You're defending in your own zone. The puck is in the corner behind your net. Your assignment is an attacker near the half-wall on the opposite side. Do you watch the puck or watch your man?
badOpt: Crash the net for a rebound since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover again.
correctOpt: Watch your man — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
why: In man coverage, losing sight of your assignment is how they get open for a pass. Keep them in front of you and use your peripheral vision for the puck.

sit: You're forechecking. The D picks up the puck behind the net and starts to walk out to the left. You're closing from the right. Your linemate is on the left. How do you split?
badOpt: Take the outside lane since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: You force the D's direction from the right, your linemate cuts off the outlet to the left — trap them between you.
why: A two-player forecheck trap works by forcing the puck carrier in a direction while the other player cuts off the outlet in that direction.

sit: Your coach calls a timeout with 30 seconds left and your team down by one. You're going back on after the timeout. What should be the first thing in your mind?
badOpt: Challenge at the line since the other team has committed to the other side on the play.
correctOpt: Know your specific job for the next 30 seconds — faceoff role, where to go, who to cover.
why: A timeout is a chance to execute a specific play. Walking back on the ice with a clear assignment is more valuable than general energy.

sit: You're in the offensive zone. You can shoot from where you are — a decent but not great angle. OR you can pass to a teammate in the slot who's slightly covered but would have a much better shot. What do you do?
badOpt: Skate to the corner and dig it out because your teammates need time to set up on the play.
correctOpt: Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk.
why: A slightly covered player in the slot still represents a higher-danger chance than a clean shot from a poor angle. The math of chance quality favors the slot, even with some risk.

sit: Your team has been in the offensive zone for 45 seconds. The other team's defensemen are tired. Your team wins a puck battle and the D are caught flat-footed. What do you do?
badOpt: Drop back and play defense since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Attack immediately through the neutral zone — tired defensemen flat-footed after a long shift are vulnerable to a fast attack.
why: Tired, flat-footed defenders after a long shift in their own zone are vulnerable to a fast transition. Attacking immediately — even if your team is also tired — exploits the window before it closes.

sit: You skate into the offensive zone with speed. You planned to shoot from the right side, but as you enter you see the goalie has that side covered and a teammate is breaking to the net on the left. What do you do?
badOpt: Chip it past them since the other team has committed to the other side while staying in position.
correctOpt: Read and react — pass to your breaking teammate. The plan changes when the situation changes.
why: Plans are starting points, not locks. If the situation changes — a breaking teammate with the goalie covering your planned shot is a change — read it and react.

sit: A skilled forward has beaten your forecheck and is coming at you with a step of speed. You can't catch them before they enter the zone. What's your priority?
badOpt: Skate hard since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover right away.
correctOpt: Get to the inside position and your lane — angle them toward the boards and stay between them and the net.
why: When you can't catch a fast attacker, getting to the inside position and your lane takes away their shooting angle and forces them to go wide. Your goalie can handle the angle.

sit: You're low in the offensive zone with the puck. The D at the point just stepped down from the blue line because they saw a lane. They're now inside the zone at the top of the circles. What's the play?
badOpt: Drive to the net front because your teammates need time to set up again.
correctOpt: Pass to the D who stepped in — they now have a shot from a dangerous area.
why: A defenseman stepping inside the dots has created a scoring chance. Hitting them in stride while inside the dots is a high-danger play.

sit: Your penalty kill is in a box. The power play's center has dropped below the dots to create a 1-3-1. Your box is now mismatched against their formation. What adjustment do you make?
badOpt: Change lines since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
correctOpt: Adjust — one PK forward drops to pressure the center who dropped in. The box has to mirror the threat.
why: A 1-3-1 power play vs. a box requires a PK adjustment. One forward drops to pressure the center who's now below the dots — leaving them open in that area gives up the most dangerous shots in hockey.

sit: Your team is defending. The goalie makes a save and controls the puck. The other team has three forecheckers in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout option?
badOpt: Dump it in since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover from this spot.
correctOpt: Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player.
why: After a save under forecheck pressure, the goalie needs to read the breakout. Finding the center or an escaping winger breaks the forecheck immediately.

sit: The other team takes a shot and your goalie makes the save. The rebound goes to the corner. An opposing player is racing to it. What do you do?
badOpt: Pass back since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover from this spot.
correctOpt: Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck.
why: Beating the opposing player to the rebound — or at minimum getting inside position — prevents the second shot. Letting them get a clean rebound in the corner means a second chance against you.

sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck for what you think will be a better shot. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
badOpt: Protect the puck there since the other team has committed to the other side on the play.
correctOpt: Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.

sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1. You've taken away the pass. The shooter pulls up and shoots. The goalie makes the save but the rebound comes right to the second attacker. What do you do?
badOpt: Hold the puck since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Get your body in front of the second attacker — between them and the net — before they can get the shot off.
why: When a rebound goes to the second attacker, your job shifts from pass prevention to body blocking. Getting your body between them and the net before they shoot is the only play.

sit: You're in the offensive zone with 5 seconds left in a tied game. You have the puck at the half-wall. A teammate is breaking to the net — open but a defender is almost there. You have a shooting lane. One second to decide. What do you do?
badOpt: Drop back and play defense since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Pass to the breaking teammate — they'll get there before the defender and an open player at the net beats your half-wall shot.
why: In a tied game with 5 seconds left, a breaking teammate who'll beat the defender to the net is a higher-danger chance than a half-wall shot. The risk is the pass, the reward is the highest-danger chance on the ice. That's the elite read.

sit: The puck is in the corner to your right. You're set on the post. Before the pass comes out, what should you be doing with your eyes?
badOpt: Take the outside lane since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Scan the slot and weak side to locate the other attackers before the puck moves.
why: Pre-scanning while the puck is still — finding the backdoor player, the high slot shooter, the trailer — is what separates elite goalies. When the puck moves, you already know where the danger is.

sit: The puck is tight to your right post, below the goal line — a sharp-angle walkout threat. What's the best way to seal the post?
badOpt: Skate to the corner and dig it out since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Go into Reverse VH (RVH) — pad stacked against the post, blocker and glove up to cover the top.
why: RVH seals the bottom of the net against the post while keeping the top covered with the blocker and glove. It's the modern answer to walkouts and goal-line plays. Standing tall gives up the bottom.

sit: There's a point shot coming and two players — one from each team — are in your sightline. You can only see flashes of the puck. What do you do?
badOpt: Pass back since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover and look for support.
correctOpt: Lower your stance slightly to find a gap, track the puck with micro-movements, and prepare for a tip or deflection.
why: Screens aren't all-or-nothing. A slight drop in stance, moving your head to find a sightline, and tracking through small gaps keeps you in the save. Expect tips — the shot won't necessarily arrive at the expected angle.

sit: The puck is rimming around the boards and will end up behind your net. Two opposing forwards are forechecking hard. What do you do?
badOpt: Cut to the middle of the ice since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Get to the puck first and either rim it to your D's forehand on the weak side, or freeze it if your D can't get there.
why: A goalie who plays the puck well under forecheck pressure is a huge weapon. Beat the forecheckers to the puck and either set up your D on the open side or freeze it to relieve pressure. Passive goalies let forechecks succeed.

sit: A skilled forward is coming on a breakaway. They're moving fast. What's your strategy before they reach the top of the circles?
badOpt: Set up behind the net and wait since the other team has committed to the other side.
correctOpt: Stay patient on your feet, cut the angle at the top of the crease, and make them commit first.
why: Patience wins breakaways. Staying on your feet as long as possible forces the shooter to show you their move first. Committing early — going down or lunging — gives the shooter the easy side.

sit: The puck is passed from the left point to the right half-wall. You're set for the point shot and need to get square to the new shooter. What's the right movement?
badOpt: Dump it in since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover at that point.
correctOpt: T-push hard in your stance — stay on your feet because the shot isn't immediate and you need to be ready to track.
why: The movement type depends on the situation. For a pass to a new shooting location where the shot isn't imminent, stay on your feet with a T-push. Reserve butterfly slides for when the shot is coming immediately and you need to drop.

sit: You're making a save from a shot on the right side. You can control where the rebound goes with your pads or blocker. What's the best target for the rebound?
badOpt: Pass back because your teammates need time to set up.
correctOpt: Into the left corner — away from the shooter and out of the slot.
why: Rebound direction is an active skill. Angling your pad to redirect the rebound to the far corner takes it out of the most dangerous area. A rebound in the slot is a goal waiting to happen.

sit: A shooter is coming down the right wing. You know from previous shifts they always shoot short side, top corner on the rush. What's your approach?
badOpt: Stay in the neutral zone since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Cheat slightly to take away their preferred shot — respect their tendency by giving a bit more of the opposite corner.
why: Elite goalies track shooter tendencies within a game. If a player has shown you their preferred spot multiple times, slightly cheating to take it away forces them to beat you somewhere else — often a spot they're less comfortable with.

sit: It's a 3-on-2 rush. Your D are in decent position. The puck carrier is on the right side. Where should you set up?
badOpt: Set up behind the net and wait since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover and look for support.
correctOpt: Slightly cheat right to match the puck carrier while staying square — trust your D on the pass, but give yourself the best angle on the shot.
why: On an odd-man rush, the goalie cheats slightly to the puck side to take away the shot while trusting the D to handle passes. Dead center gives up too much on both sides. Cheating hard commits too much.

sit: The puck is shot around the boards behind your net. A forechecker is closing from your left. Your D is coming to the left side. Your other D is wide open on the right side. What's the best play?
badOpt: Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover from this spot.
correctOpt: Stop it, then make a short pass to your right D who is wide open on the weak side — a clean D-to-D breakout under pressure.
why: A goalie who can play a D-to-D pass under pressure is a major asset. Identifying the uncovered defender and getting them the puck clean starts the breakout before the forecheck sets up. This is an advanced skill worth developing.

sit: An attacker is skating fast behind your net from right to left, setting up a wraparound attempt on your left post. What do you do?
badOpt: Swing wide along the boards since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover before they score.
correctOpt: Push off your right post and move with them, arriving at the left post before they do — seal the bottom of the post with your pad.
why: On a wraparound, the race to the post is everything. Moving with the puck carrier and sealing the near post before they arrive eliminates the wrap option. Late arrival = goal.

sit: Your team is down by two goals with three minutes left. The other team gets a rush chance. What should your positioning look like?
badOpt: Crash the net for a rebound because your teammates need time to set up.
correctOpt: More aggressive than usual — come out further to cut angles and make them beat you.
why: When trailing late, goalies can and should play slightly more aggressively to make stops — because every save gives your team more time. But this is situational: elite goalies adjust their depth based on game state, not just the shooter.

sit: You just gave up a soft goal. Your body language is visibly frustrated. There's a faceoff at center ice in 15 seconds. What's the most important thing to do?
badOpt: Try to stickhandle through them since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
correctOpt: Reset immediately — physical cue (tap posts, breathe, focus on the next shot) and put the goal behind you. Analyze later, not now.
why: Mental reset after a goal is a real skill. Elite goalies have a consistent physical cue — tap the posts, deep breath, water bottle — to clear the last save and focus on the next shot. Dwelling on a bad goal during the game causes the next one.
